title: Vanishing cycles and bilinear forms
speaker: Will Sawin
abstract: In joint work with Emmanuel Kowalski and Philippe Michel, we prove two different estimates on sums of coefficients of modular forms---one related to L-functions and another to the level of distribution. A key step in the argument is a careful analysis of vanishing cycles, a tool originally developed by Lefschetz to study the topology of algebraic varieties. We will explain why this is helpful for these problems.

proof_stages.label: Number-theoretic applications motivating the method
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with two applications: power-saving asymptotics for moments and correlations of central values of twists of modular-form L-functions, and a beyond-square-root level-of-distribution result for the convolution coefficients lambda_f * 1 in prime moduli.

proof_stages.label: Bilinear Kloosterman-sum estimate as the common tool
proof_stages.summary: A normalized hyper-Kloosterman sum over finite fields is defined, and the central bilinear estimate is stated: arbitrary coefficient functions alpha and beta can be summed against Kloosterman sums with a power saving over the trivial L2 bound in ranges slightly below the square-root barrier.

proof_stages.label: Reduction of modular-form sums to bilinear Kloosterman forms
proof_stages.summary: The level-of-distribution problem is smoothed and decomposed, congruences are detected by additive characters, and Voronoi/Poisson transformations convert these additive characters into Kloosterman sums, while the L-function moment problem is similarly reduced using approximate functional equations and character orthogonality.

proof_stages.label: Cauchy, shift-by-ab, and Hölder reduction
proof_stages.summary: To prove the bilinear estimate, the Kloosterman factor is separated from arbitrary alpha and beta by Cauchy-Schwarz, a shift-by-ab averaging trick, a change of variables, and Hölder's inequality, reducing the analytic estimate to a fourth-moment complete-sum problem.

proof_stages.label: Complete Kloosterman correlation lemma
proof_stages.summary: The required fourth moment is packaged into functions built from products of four Kloosterman sums, and the key complete correlation bound asserts orthogonality up to O(q^{3/2}) outside a bounded-degree exceptional algebraic set.

proof_stages.label: Trace-function and Lefschetz cohomological strategy
proof_stages.summary: Complete exponential sums are rewritten as trace functions of l-adic sheaves, built from Kummer, Artin-Schreier, and summation operations, and the Lefschetz trace formula plus Deligne's Riemann hypothesis reduces square-root cancellation to controlling the top compactly supported cohomology term.

proof_stages.label: Top cohomology as a monodromy-invariant problem
proof_stages.summary: Excision and Poincare duality identify the relevant H_c^2 with invariants of a pi_1-representation, so the desired correlation estimate is reduced to proving irreducibility and pairwise distinctness of the sheaves associated with different parameters.

proof_stages.label: Vanishing cycles, local monodromy, and family irreducibility
proof_stages.summary: The irreducibility is proved by studying local monodromy of Kloosterman sheaves via vanishing cycles, Katz's local computations, and a family argument using Deligne semicontinuity to pass from an irreducible total family to the relevant fibers.

examples.name: Ramanujan delta as a level-one cusp form

examples.engagement_reason: the delta function is only offered as a familiar example of a level-one cusp form, with no computation or property checked on it
